problem.title: Renako Amaori and XOR Game (hard version)

sources.notes: Hard version of the two-player XOR game.
Official editorial reduces the hard version to the most significant bit of the XOR of all numbers.

annotation.summary: Determine the winner of a two-player game where each pair contributes to final XOR scores.
annotation.constraints: The input size is linear, so the game outcome must be derived from an invariant rather than simulated over strategies.
annotation.core_idea: The XOR of all 2n numbers is invariant and equals the XOR difference between final scores. If it is zero the game ties; otherwise the most significant set bit determines the comparison, and the last pair whose two values differ in that bit decides which player can force the win.
annotation.complexity: O(n log A) per test case.

solution_variants.name: highest-bit-game-invariant
solution_variants.summary: Compute global XOR, find its highest set bit, then locate the last pair that differs on this bit to decide the winner.

tags.evidence: The task is solved by proving which player controls the final decisive move under optimal play.
The winner is determined by the XOR invariant and the most significant set bit.
Only the highest set bit of the final XOR matters for comparing scores.
tags.description: Use the most significant differing bit to decide ordering, winner, or lexicographic/numeric comparison.
tags.created_reason: This recurring bitwise comparison trick is more specific than the generic bitmask tag.
